_comment: Configuration for Maproom MCP Server in VS Code

setup: 1. Install the MCP extension for VS Code from the marketplace
2. Build the Maproom MCP server: cd packages/maproom-mcp && pnpm build
3. Replace '/absolute/path/to/maproom-mcp' with the actual absolute path to your maproom-mcp package
4. Update MAPROOM_DATABASE_URL with your PostgreSQL connection string
5. Ensure PostgreSQL is running and the maproom database is initialized
6. Index your repository using the maproom CLI before using the MCP server
7. Save this configuration to your VS Code MCP settings

method_1_settings_json.note: Merge this configuration under the 'mcp.servers' key in settings.json

MAPROOM_DATABASE_URL.description: PostgreSQL connection string for Maproom database

LOG_LEVEL.description: Logging level for MCP server (trace, debug, info, warn, error)

MAPROOM_MCP_LOG_FILE.description: Path to log file for debugging (optional)

available_tools.name: status
available_tools.use_case: Start here to see what repositories and worktrees are indexed
available_tools.example: Call status tool without parameters to see all indexed repos

available_tools.name: search
available_tools.use_case: Find code by concept or functionality

available_tools.name: open
available_tools.use_case: Retrieve code from files found in search results
available_tools.example: Use relpath and worktree from search results

available_tools.name: context
available_tools.description: Get contextual code around a chunk
available_tools.use_case: Understand code in context with related functions and imports
available_tools.example: Use chunk_id from search results to get surrounding context

available_tools.name: upsert
available_tools.example: Index specific files or entire directories

available_tools.name: explain
available_tools.use_case: Get comprehensive documentation for a code symbol
available_tools.example: Use chunk_id from search results to explain a function or class

command_palette.description: Access MCP tools through VS Code Command Palette

inline_integration.description: Some MCP extensions provide inline code actions

output_panel.description: View MCP server logs and tool results

server_not_starting: Check the MCP extension is installed and enabled
Verify the path to dist/index.js is absolute and correct
Check VS Code Output panel (View > Output > MCP) for errors
Ensure Node.js is in your PATH
Try reloading VS Code window (Cmd/Ctrl+Shift+P > Reload Window)
connection_errors: Ensure PostgreSQL is running and accessible
Check firewall settings for database connections
Test database connection outside VS Code first
tools_not_working: Use 'status' tool first to verify repository is indexed
Check that worktree names match what's in the index
Verify search queries are simple (1-3 words work best)
Check MCP server logs for detailed error messages
performance_issues: Check database query performance in PostgreSQL logs

workspace_configuration: Commit .vscode/settings.json (without sensitive credentials) to share with team
Use environment variables for sensitive data like MAPROOM_DATABASE_URL
development_workflow: Start with 'status' tool to verify what's indexed
Use simple search terms (1-3 words) for best results
Use 'open' tool to view code from search results
Use 'context' tool to understand code relationships
Re-index with 'upsert' after making significant changes
team_collaboration: Share consistent database configurations across team
Document required environment variables in README
Use shared PostgreSQL instance for team-wide code search
Index main branches and active feature branches

steps: 1. Call 'status' tool to verify repo is indexed
2. Call 'search' with query='authentication' and repo='your-repo'
3. Review search results for relevant chunks
4. Call 'open' with relpath and worktree from best result
5. View the code in VS Code

context_exploration.description: Understand how a function is used
steps: 1. Call 'search' to find the function of interest
2. Get chunk_id from search results
3. Call 'context' with chunk_id and budget_tokens=8000
4. Review the context bundle showing callers, callees, and tests
5. Use 'open' on related chunks to explore further
1. Make code changes in your repository
2. Call 'upsert' with paths to changed files
3. Provide current commit hash (or 'HEAD')
4. Verify with 'search' that new code is findable
5. Continue development with up-to-date search index
1. Search for 'main entry index' to find entry points
2. Use 'context' to see what the entry point imports
3. Search for key architectural terms (router, controller, service)
4. Use 'explain' tool to get detailed symbol documentation
5. Build mental model of codebase architecture
